title: Windows-safe path encoding for prose files with collision handling
purpose: Provides functions to escape and unescape paths for use in a Windows-safe manner, preventing reserved names and forbidden characters while handling collisions with SHA1 hashes.
module_context: Exports interfaces and functions for path escaping, including handling of reserved Windows names, forbidden characters, trailing dots/spaces, and collision suffixes.

steps.content: Generate a SHA1 hash of the input string.
Return the first 8 characters of the hexadecimal digest.
Extract the stem of the basename before any dot.
Check if the uppercase stem matches any reserved Windows name.
Return true if a match is found, false otherwise.
If the segment is empty, '.', or '..', return it unchanged.
Replace all forbidden characters with underscores and track changes.
If the segment ends with dots or spaces, replace them with underscores and track changes.
If the segment is a reserved Windows name, append an underscore and track changes.
Return the escaped segment and a flag indicating whether any changes occurred.
Split the source path into segments using forward or backward slashes.
Escape each segment individually and check if any changes occurred.
If no changes occurred, return the path with backslashes replaced by forward slashes.
Generate a short hash of the original source path.
Modify the last segment to include the hash as a collision suffix.
Join the escaped segments with forward slashes and return the result.
If a mapping is provided and the escaped path exists in it, return the mapped value.
Remove any collision suffixes from the escaped path.
Split the path into segments using forward slashes.
If the last segment ends with an underscore, attempt to restore the original name.
If the last segment has a dot and the stem is a reserved name, restore the original name.
Join the segments back together with forward slashes and return the result.
If the input is already a clean POSIX relative path, return it unchanged.
Detect if the input is a Windows absolute path.
Resolve the input path relative to the project if it's not absolute.
Compute the relative path from the project to the resolved input.
Replace backslashes with forward slashes in the relative path.
If the relative path starts with '..', throw an error indicating the path is outside the project.
Return the normalized POSIX relative path.
